items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: Why is a reflex action faster than a conscious response?
items.correct: The signal does not need to be processed by the brain — it travels through a simpler reflex arc via the spinal cord.
items.misconception: The brain decides reflexes faster than other actions because it skips thinking.
items.random_wrong: Reflexes are faster because they use specially fast neurones not used elsewhere.
items.why: Students assume the brain is always in charge; they don't realise reflexes specifically bypass the brain to save time.

items.q: Why are X-linked recessive conditions more common in males?
items.correct: Males have only one X chromosome, so a single recessive allele is expressed — there's no second X to mask it.
items.misconception: X-linked recessive conditions affect more males because the Y chromosome makes the disease worse.
items.random_wrong: X-linked conditions affect only males because females cannot inherit X-linked alleles.
items.why: Students don't grasp hemizygosity — they think the Y chromosome must be active in the inheritance, instead of absent for that gene.
The answer examiners want is: thymine. “cytosine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: protein. “whole organism” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a DNA sample, 30% of bases are adenine. What % are thymine?
items.why: Exact answer: 30 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
